abstract: Bimanual dexterous grasping is a fundamental and promising area in robotics, yet its progress is constrained by the lack of comprehensive datasets and powerful generation models. In this work, we propose BiDexGrasp, consists of a large-scale bimanual dexterous grasp dataset and a novel generation model. For dataset, we propose a novel bimanual grasp synthesis pipeline to efficiently annotate physically feasible data for dataset construction. This pipeline addresses the challenges of high-dimensional bimanual grasping through a two-stage synthesis strategy of efficient region-based grasp initialization and decoupled force-closure grasp optimization. Powered by this pipeline, we construct a large-scale bimanual dexterous grasp dataset, comprising 6351 diverse objects with sizes ranging from 30 to 80 cm, along with 9.7 million annotated grasp data. Based on this dataset, we further introduce a bimanual-coordinated and geometry-size-adaptive dexterous grasping generation framework. The framework lies in two key designs: a bimanual coordination module and a geometry-size-adaptive grasp generation strategy to generate coordinated and high-quality grasps on unseen objects. Extensive experiments conducted in both simulation and real world demonstrate the superior performance of our proposed data synthesis pipeline and learned generative framework.

citations.fullCitation: [26] Yanming Shao and Chenxi Xiao. Bimanual grasp synthesis for dexterous robot hands. IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters, 2024.
citations.justification: This paper is the primary point of comparison for the BiDexGrasp data synthesis pipeline. The authors explicitly state they compare their results against BimanGrasp, identifying it as the "only publicly available open-source bimanual grasp synthesis pipeline" and demonstrating significant improvements in grasp success rate and synthesis speed.
citations.title: Bimanual grasp synthesis for dexterous robot hands

citations.fullCitation: [2] Jiayi Chen, Yubin Ke, and He Wang. Bodex: Scalable and efficient robotic dexterous grasp synthesis using bilevel optimization. In 2025 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA), pages 01–08. IEEE, 2025.
citations.justification: This work is foundational to the methodology of the main paper. The BiDexGrasp synthesis pipeline is described as an extension of the framework proposed in BoDex, and the baseline for the ablation studies is a direct bimanual adaptation of this single-hand synthesis method.
citations.title: Bodex: Scalable and efficient robotic dexterous grasp synthesis using bilevel optimization

citations.fullCitation: [30] Ruicheng Wang, Jialiang Zhang, Jiayi Chen, Yinzhen Xu, Puhao Li, Tengyu Liu, and He Wang. Dexgraspnet: A large-scale robotic dexterous grasp dataset for general objects based on simulation. arXiv preprint arXiv:2210.02697, 2022.
citations.justification: DexGraspNet is a major large-scale single-hand dexterous grasping dataset that provides crucial context for the contributions of BiDexGrasp. The paper uses objects from DexGraspNet and directly compares its dataset statistics (e.g., number of objects, grasp count) against it in Table I to highlight its own scale and advancements in the bimanual domain.
citations.title: Dexgraspnet: A large-scale robotic dexterous grasp dataset for general objects based on simulation

citations.fullCitation: [17] Quanzhou Li, Zhonghua Wu, Jingbo Wang, Chen Change Loy, and Bo Dai. Dhagrasp: Synthesizing affordance-aware dual-hand grasps with text instructions. arXiv preprint arXiv:2509.22175, 2025.
citations.justification: This is another key contemporary work on bimanual grasp synthesis that helps establish the state of the art. The BiDexGrasp paper compares its dataset against DHAGrasp in Table I to differentiate its contributions, particularly concerning the diversity of object sizes and the total number of grasps.
citations.title: Dhagrasp: Synthesizing affordance-aware dual-hand grasps with text instructions

intermediateReport:
## Research Paper Report: BiDexGrasp: Coordinated Bimanual Dexterous Grasps across Object Geometries and Sizes

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The research paper "BiDexGrasp: Coordinated Bimanual Dexterous Grasps across Object Geometries and Sizes" was authored by:

*   **Mu Lin**: School of Computer Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University
*   **Yi-Lin Wei**: School of Computer Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University
*   **Jiaxuan Chen**: School of Computer Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University
*   **Yuhao Lin**: School of Computer Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University
*   **Shuoyu Chen**: School of Computer Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University
*   **Jiangran Lyu**: School of Computer Science, Peking University
*   **Jiayi Chen**: School of Computer Science, Peking University
*   **Yansong Tang**: Shenzhen International Graduate School, Tsinghua University
*   **He Wang**: School of Computer Science, Peking University
*   **Wei-Shi Zheng**: School of Computer Science and Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University (Corresponding author)

### 2. How this Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Robotic dexterous grasping is a field focused on enabling robots to interact with real-world objects using multi-fingered hands, mimicking human manipulation capabilities. Within this field, data-driven methods have gained prominence, necessitating the creation of large-scale, high-quality datasets and robust generation models.

Previous research in dexterous grasping has primarily concentrated on single-hand operations. This includes work on grasp synthesis methods based on force-closure principles and data-driven generative models. However, the real world presents objects of varying sizes and geometries that often exceed the capabilities of single-hand grasping, highlighting the potential utility of bimanual dexterous grasping.

Existing studies addressing bimanual dexterous grasping have largely extended single-hand grasp pipelines. These approaches often produce datasets with limited diversity in object sizes and geometries. Furthermore, both direct transfer of state-of-the-art single-hand methods and application of current bimanual methods have shown suboptimal performance when confronted with objects of diverse scales and complex geometries. This limitation is attributed to the increased complexity of the bimanual search space, where the number of initialization candidates can grow quadratically, and force-closure optimization occurs in a high-dimensional space, affecting both efficiency and grasp quality. Some bimanual methods rely on reinforcement learning, which faces scalability challenges and dependence on well-designed reward functions. Other data-driven frameworks adapted from single-hand models are often constrained by dataset quality and generative architecture limitations.

The present work contributes to the advancement of bimanual dexterous grasping by addressing these identified gaps. It introduces a comprehensive solution comprising a large-scale, diverse dataset and a novel generation model specifically designed to tackle the challenges of varied object sizes, geometries, and the high-dimensional bimanual action space. This positions the work as a development aimed at enhancing the generalization and physical feasibility of bimanual grasping in complex, real-world scenarios.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objectives of this research are twofold:
1.  **To construct a large-scale, high-quality bimanual dexterous grasp dataset:** This objective is motivated by the recognized lack of comprehensive datasets in bimanual grasping that adequately cover objects with diverse geometries and sizes. Existing datasets are noted to have limitations in terms of scale variation, often restricting the applicability of learned models. The authors aim to overcome the challenges associated with efficiently annotating physically feasible bimanual grasp data, particularly given the high-dimensional nature of bimanual grasping. This involves addressing issues of increased complexity and reduced efficiency in the data synthesis process.
2.  **To develop a novel bimanual-coordinated and geometry-size-adaptive dexterous grasp generation framework:** This objective stems from the observation that current bimanual grasping methods perform inadequately on objects with diverse geometries and scales. The motivation is to create a data-driven model that can learn from a diverse dataset and generate coordinated, high-quality grasp poses for unseen objects. The framework needs to explicitly address two key challenges:
    *   **Bimanual coordination:** Ensuring that the two hands work collaboratively to achieve stable grasps and avoid collisions.
    *   **Adaptation across object geometries and sizes:** Enabling the model to generalize effectively to objects with significant variations in shape and scale, which typically hinder efficient model learning and increase the complexity of feature extraction and action space.

By achieving these objectives, the research aims to provide a foundational resource (the dataset) and an advanced tool (the generation framework) that collectively advance the capabilities of robotic bimanual dexterous grasping, making it more practical and robust for real-world applications involving diverse objects.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology employed in this research encompasses two main components: the construction of a large-scale bimanual dexterous grasp dataset and the development of a data-driven generation framework.

#### 4.1. BiDexGrasp Dataset Construction

The dataset, named BiDexGrasp, comprises 6,351 diverse objects sourced from Objaverse and DexGraspNet datasets. These objects were filtered to exclude flat geometries unsuitable for tabletop grasping. To enhance scale diversity, each object was scaled to 11 discrete sizes, ranging from 30 cm to 80 cm. The dataset contains 9.7 million annotated grasp data.

The data synthesis pipeline for BiDexGrasp consists of three stages: initialization, optimization, and filtering. The pipeline leverages the cuRobo framework for GPU-parallelized grasp synthesis.

1.  **Bimanual Region-Constraint Grasp Initialization:**
    *   **Challenge Addressed:** The quadratic growth in sampling complexity and the low success rate of random initializations for bimanual systems.
    *   **GWS-based Bimanual Grasping Region Selection:** This module identifies promising bimanual regions on an object based on their potential for stable and physically feasible grasps. It involves four steps:
        1.  Farthest Point Sampling (FPS) to select 200 anchors, with 256 points sampled within an 8 cm neighborhood around each anchor to define candidate grasping regions.
        2.  Exclusion of concave regions and region pairs with insufficient inter-region distance to ensure geometric feasibility.
        3.  Evaluation of grasp stability for remaining region pairs using the Grasp Wrench Boundary (GWB) estimated by the TDG estimator. Stability is quantified by the minimum projection of the GWB onto six orthogonal disturbance wrenches.
        4.  Retention of the top 40 region pairs based on their stability scores for subsequent initialization.
    *   **Region-based Grasp Initialization:** For selected regions, initial grasp configurations are generated. Palm positions are determined by the point on the dilated object convex hull closest to the grasping region, with orientation defined by the opposite surface normal. In-plane rotation is guided by a predefined ideal direction. Finger joint angles are initialized to an open posture and then slightly closed to conform to the object surface.
    *   **Bimanual Feasibility Verification:** Initialized grasp configurations undergo collision checking (self-collisions, inter-hand, hand-object, hand-environment) and inverse kinematics (IK) solution for both arms to confirm collision-free reachability. Unfeasible grasps are discarded.

2.  **Decoupled Grasping Optimization:**
    *   **Challenge Addressed:** The complexity of bimanual optimization due to an expanded search space and tightly coupled objectives, which can lead to unbalanced solutions where one hand dominates stability.
    *   **Decoupled Force Closure:** The optimization problem minimizes a QP-Energy function decomposed into separate energy terms for each hand, promoting stable local contacts for both. The overall objective function is defined as:
        `min(G_bi) (w_Q-left * Q(G_left) + w_Q-right * Q(G_right) + w_dis * E_dis + w_region * E_region)`
        subject to joint limits and collision-avoidance constraints.
        *   `Q(G_left)` and `Q(G_right)` are QP-energy terms for individual hands, promoting force closure.
        *   `E_dis` encourages hand contact points to be close to the object surface.
        *   `E_region` is a region consistency term, penalizing contact points that stray from the initial contact regions to maintain coordinated bimanual grasping.

3.  **Kinematics-Aware Arm-Hand Configuration Synthesis:**
    *   To ensure practical feasibility, the framework supports solving inverse kinematics (IK) for wrist poses, translating floating grasps to tabletop configurations.
    *   A pre-grasp pose is synthesized by maintaining a 1cm contact distance.
    *   A squeeze pose is calculated as the execution target.
    *   Arm joint trajectories from pre-grasp to squeeze and lift are generated.

#### 4.2. BiDexGrasp Generation Framework

The data-driven framework, also named BiDexGrasp, takes an object point cloud as input and aims to generate bimanual dexterous grasp poses. It is designed to address bimanual coordination and adaptation across diverse geometries and sizes.

1.  **Bimanual Coordination Module (BCM):**
    *   **Challenge Addressed:** The need for collaborative targeting of optimal grasp views to ensure global stability and avoid inter-hand collisions.
    *   This module estimates coordination-aware grasping views. It uniformly samples K discrete approach directions on the object's bounding sphere, creating a candidate view set.
    *   The module predicts a primary grasping view and a second view most compatible with the primary view using view probabilistic regression. Learnable view embeddings are used to extract view features via cross-attention.
    *   Bi-view features are constructed by modeling interactions between candidate views.
    *   Probabilities for single views (`p_i`) and bi-views (`p_bi_i,j`) are predicted and trained using L2 losses against ground-truth probabilities derived from successful grasps in the dataset.

2.  **Geometry-Size-Adaptive Grasp Generation Strategy:**
    *   **Challenge Addressed:** The difficulty of efficient model learning and expanded action space due to vast diversity in object sizes and geometries.
    *   **Scale-Adaptive Grasp Anchor (SAGA):** Grasp wrist prediction is formulated as a relative pose (`Δt`, `Δr`) prediction with respect to a dynamically determined grasp anchor (`g_anchor`). The anchor is adapted to the object's size and grasp view, being defined by the intersection of the predicted view with the object's minimum bounding sphere. This converts the absolute pose prediction to a relative regression task, reducing the search space.
    *   **Geometry-Adaptive Object Feature (GAF):** To adapt to object geometry, local geometric features are extracted around each predicted grasp anchor. The global point cloud is upsampled, and features are aggregated from K-nearest neighbors around each anchor, capturing fine-grained surface geometry and filtering irrelevant structures. These local features are concatenated with view and global features to form `F_ga-obj`.
    *   **Relative Dexterous Grasp Diffusion:** A Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM) is employed to generate bimanual relative wrist poses (`Δt_bi`, `Δr_bi`) and dexterous joint poses (`q_bi`).
        *   The sampling process is formalized as `p_θ(G_bi,rela_0:T | F_ga-obj)`.
        *   Training involves L2 losses for translation, rotation, and joint parameters, a Chamfer loss for hand geometry supervision, and a physics-based loss to penalize hand-object/intra-hand penetration while encouraging non-penetrating contact.

The framework's training involves the Adam optimizer with a batch size of 128 and a cosine learning rate schedule.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research presents experimental results demonstrating the performance of both the BiDexGrasp data synthesis pipeline and the BiDexGrasp generation framework in simulation and real-world environments.

#### 5.1. Data Synthesis Experiments

*   **Comparison with Prior Work (BimanGrasp):** The BiDexGrasp data synthesis pipeline was compared against BimanGrasp, an existing bimanual grasp synthesis pipeline.
    *   On the DexGraspNet dataset, BiDexGrasp achieved a grasp success rate (SUC) of 75.84% (floating hand) and 77.11% (tabletop with arm), which is approximately 2.8 times higher than BimanGrasp's SUC of 26.8%.
    *   The synthesis speed for BiDexGrasp was reported as 6.73 grasps/s (floating) and 6.87 grasps/s (tabletop), representing a roughly 40-fold speedup compared to BimanGrasp's 0.16 grasps/s.
    *   On the more complex Objaverse dataset, BiDexGrasp maintained high SUC (70.82% floating, 62.41% tabletop) and efficiency (4.55 grasps/s floating, 5.10 grasps/s tabletop), outperforming BimanGrasp's 30.21% SUC and 0.16 grasps/s speed.
*   **Ablation Study of Synthesis Pipeline:**
    *   The baseline, a straightforward extension of a single-hand method, yielded SUC-L (lift success rate) of 28.33% and SUC-A (arm-controlled lift success rate) of 12.89%.
    *   Adding Region initialization (R) increased SUC-L to 44.43% and SUC-A to 39.79%, while also improving success generation speed (SGS).
    *   Further incorporating Decoupled force-closure (Dc) increased SUC-L to 57.66% and SUC-A to 49.52%, with SGS rising to 3.96 grasps/s.
    *   The full pipeline, including pre-grasp strategies (Ps), achieved the highest SUC-L of 80.83% and SUC-A of 71.34%, with an SGS of 5.55 grasps/s. All components contributed to higher success rates and synthesis speed.

#### 5.2. Generation Framework Experiments

*   **Comparison with State-of-the-Art Methods:** The BiDexGrasp framework was evaluated against SceneDiffuser and DGTR.
    *   BiDexGrasp achieved a SUC-L of 66.78%, which was higher than SceneDiffuser (15.17%) and DGTR (41.45%).
    *   It demonstrated lower object penetration depth (PD) of 1.53 cm and self-penetration depth (SPD) of 0.01 cm, indicating more precise and stable grasps.
    *   The grasp diversity (D) of BiDexGrasp was 0.197%, which was slightly lower than DGTR (0.201%) but higher than SceneDiffuser (0.174%).
*   **Ablation Study of Generation Framework:**
    *   A baseline DDPM-based bimanual grasp generation without specific modules achieved a SUC-L of 41.17%.
    *   Including the Bimanual Coordination Module (BCM) and Geometry-Adaptive Feature (GAF) but without Scale-Adaptive Grasp Anchor (SAGA) resulted in a SUC-L of 52.74%.
    *   The combination of BCM, GAF, and SAGA yielded the highest SUC-L of 61.93%, along with improved PD, SPD, and Contact Distance Consistency (CDC). This indicates the complementary contributions of each module.

#### 5.3. Efficiency Analysis of Framework

*   The average inference time for BiDexGrasp was 1.67 ± 0.05 seconds per batch (batch size 64), compared to the baseline's 1.21 ± 0.03 seconds. This indicates that the framework improves grasp success rate with a moderate increase in inference time.

#### 5.4. Generalization to Other Dexterous Hands

*   The data synthesis pipeline was shown to be adaptable to different hand models, with visualizations demonstrating its application to the Leap Hand, producing grasp samples.

#### 5.5. Failure Case Analysis

*   Observed failure cases primarily involved physical instability, such as minor object penetration or floating grasps when the generative model did not perfectly balance penetration penalties and contact supervision.
*   Some objects experienced lateral sliding due to insufficient force closure, leading to grasp failure.

#### 5.6. Real-World Experiments

*   The framework was deployed on two robotic platforms: a Brainco hand with a Piper arm and an Inspire hand with Unitree G1 robots.
*   RGB-D observations from a Realsense camera were used for object mesh reconstruction via Hyper3D, with scale determination by bounding box alignment.
*   Generated Shadow Hand poses were retargeted to the Brainco or Inspire hands.
*   Experiments on unseen real-world objects of various sizes and geometries, with six grasp attempts per object, showed success rates ranging from 33.3% to 100% across different objects and hands (Table VII), indicating the framework's ability to generate high-quality bimanual dexterous grasps in physical environments.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The work presented in BiDexGrasp contributes a two-pronged solution to advance the field of bimanual dexterous grasping in robotics. Its significance lies in addressing critical limitations prevalent in existing research, particularly concerning data availability and model generalization.

Firstly, the development of a novel bimanual grasp synthesis pipeline offers a more efficient and reliable method for generating physically feasible and high-quality grasp annotations. This pipeline, characterized by its region-based initialization and decoupled force-closure optimization, demonstrates improved success rates and synthesis speed compared to prior methods. The resulting large-scale BiDexGrasp dataset, comprising 6,351 diverse objects ranging from 30 to 80 cm and 9.7 million annotated bimanual grasps, provides a substantial resource. This dataset's diversity in object geometries and scales is designed to facilitate the training of more robust and generalizable bimanual grasping models, addressing a previous scarcity of such comprehensive data.

Secondly, the proposed BiDexGrasp generation framework, built upon this dataset, introduces explicit mechanisms for bimanual coordination and geometry-size adaptation. The bimanual coordination module aims to ensure collaborative hand movements, while the geometry-size-adaptive strategy, with its scale-adaptive grasp anchors and geometry-adaptive features, enhances the framework's ability to generalize across varying object characteristics. The framework's demonstrated performance in generating coordinated and high-success-rate bimanual dexterous grasps across diverse objects, validated through extensive simulation and real-world experiments, indicates its potential for practical application.

The potential impact of this research is multifaceted. The BiDexGrasp dataset can serve as a benchmark for future studies in bimanual dexterous grasping, enabling the development and comparison of new algorithms under more realistic and challenging conditions. The generative framework offers a template for creating more capable robotic systems that can handle a wider array of objects in complex environments, moving towards human-like manipulation capabilities. This could accelerate advancements in fields such as industrial automation, logistics, assistive robotics, and domestic robotics, where handling objects of varied shapes and sizes is a common requirement. By promoting research on scalable, generalizable, and physically grounded bimanual dexterous grasping and manipulation, BiDexGrasp positions itself as a foundational step towards more adaptable and versatile robotic agents.
overview:
## The Challenge of Bimanual Dexterous Grasping

Robotic dexterous grasping aims to replicate the human ability to manipulate objects using multi-fingered hands. While significant progress has been made in single-hand grasping, many real-world objects—due to their substantial size, heavy weight, or complex geometries—require the use of two hands. Transitioning from single-hand to bimanual (two-handed) manipulation introduces several complexities. The search space for valid hand configurations grows quadratically, and the system must ensure precise coordination between both hands to maintain stability and avoid collisions.

![BiDexGrasp Generation Framework](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06589v1/x2.png)
*Figure 1: The BiDexGrasp generation framework, illustrating the Bimanual Coordination Module, Scale-Adaptive Anchor strategy, and Geometry-Adaptive Features.*

Existing approaches often struggle with objects of varying scales and shapes. Models trained on small, single-handed objects frequently fail when applied to larger items like crates or stools. Furthermore, current bimanual datasets lack the diversity and scale necessary to train robust, generalizable models. BiDexGrasp addresses these issues by providing a large-scale dataset and a generative framework specifically designed for coordinated, geometry-adaptive bimanual grasping.

## The BiDexGrasp Dataset: Diversity Across Scales

A central contribution of this research is the BiDexGrasp dataset, which encompasses 6,351 unique objects sourced from the Objaverse and DexGraspNet collections. Unlike previous datasets that might focus on a narrow range of object sizes, BiDexGrasp explicitly incorporates scale diversity. Each object in the dataset is scaled to 11 discrete sizes, ranging from 30 cm to 80 cm.

The final dataset contains 9.7 million annotated grasp instances. This scale and variety are intended to allow models to learn how grasping strategies must change as an object grows from something that might fit in a single palm to something that requires a wide, two-handed embrace. To create such a massive amount of high-quality data, the authors developed a specialized synthesis pipeline.

## High-Efficiency Data Synthesis Pipeline

Generating millions of physically feasible bimanual grasps manually is impossible, and traditional random sampling is highly inefficient. The researchers developed a three-stage automated pipeline to generate the BiDexGrasp dataset.

![Data Synthesis Pipeline](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06589v1/x1.png)
*Figure 2: The automated data synthesis pipeline featuring region-based initialization and decoupled optimization.*

### Bimanual Region-Constraint Initialization
To avoid the "needle in a haystack" problem of random initialization, the pipeline first identifies promising grasping regions on the object surface. It uses a Grasp Wrench Space (GWS) estimator to evaluate pairs of surface regions. Only region pairs that offer high potential for stable, force-closure grasps are selected for initial hand placement. This step significantly increases the success rate of the subsequent optimization.

### Decoupled Force-Closure Optimization
Once the hands are initialized, their poses are refined through optimization. A common problem in bimanual optimization is that one hand might dominate the stability score, leading to a "lazy" second hand that doesn't actually contribute to the grasp. To prevent this, the authors propose a decoupled energy function:

$$ \min_{G_{bi}} (w_{Q-left} Q(G_{left}) + w_{Q-right} Q(G_{right}) + w_{dis} E_{dis} + w_{region} E_{region}) $$

In this formulation, $Q(G_{left})$ and $Q(G_{right})$ represent the force-closure quality for the left and right hands individually. By optimizing these terms alongside a distance term $E_{dis}$ and a region consistency term $E_{region}$, the framework ensures that both hands actively participate in a stable, coordinated grasp.

### Kinematics-Aware Filtering
Finally, the synthesized "floating" hand poses are converted into realistic tabletop configurations. This involves solving inverse kinematics for robotic arms to ensure that the generated grasps are actually reachable by a physical robot on a flat surface without collisions.

## The BiDexGrasp Generation Framework

With a large-scale dataset in hand, the authors developed a generative model to predict bimanual grasps for unseen objects. The framework takes an object point cloud as input and produces coordinated hand poses. It relies on three primary modules to handle coordination, scale, and geometry.

### Bimanual Coordination Module (BCM)
The BCM ensures that the two hands work together rather than as two independent agents. It samples various "grasp views" (approaching directions) and predicts the probability $p_i$ of a single view being successful, as well as the joint probability $p_{bi,i,j}$ of a pair of views working in harmony. This allows the model to select two approach directions that are globally stable and unlikely to result in inter-hand collisions.

### Scale-Adaptive Grasp Anchor (SAGA)
Predicting the absolute position of a hand in 3D space is difficult when object sizes vary from 30 cm to 80 cm. To simplify this task, SAGA introduces a relative coordinate system. It defines a grasp anchor $g_{anchor}$ at the intersection of the chosen approach view and the object's bounding sphere. The model then predicts the relative translation $\Delta t$ and rotation $\Delta r$ of the wrist with respect to this anchor. This normalization makes the learning process much more robust to changes in object scale.

### Geometry-Adaptive Object Feature (GAF)
To handle complex shapes, the framework combines global object features with local geometric details. GAF extracts local point cloud features specifically around the predicted grasp anchors. This ensures that the model "pays attention" to the fine-grained surface geometry where the fingers will actually make contact, filtering out irrelevant parts of the object.

### Relative Dexterous Grasp Diffusion
The final hand poses are generated using a Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model (DDPM). The diffusion process iteratively refines a noise-filled hand pose into a precise configuration $G_{bi,rela,0:T}$ based on the condition $F_{ga-obj}$ (the combined global and local geometry features). The model's objective is to learn the distribution:

$$ p_{\theta}(G_{bi,rela,0:T} | F_{ga-obj}) $$

This probabilistic approach allows the model to generate a diverse set of valid grasps for any given object.

## Experimental Results and Performance

The BiDexGrasp framework was evaluated in both simulation and real-world environments, showing significant improvements over existing methods like SceneDiffuser and DGTR.

### Simulation Success Rates
In large-scale simulation tests, the proposed framework achieved a lift success rate of 66.78%, more than 25% higher than previous state-of-the-art bimanual methods. It also showed much lower object penetration (1.53 cm) and self-penetration (0.01 cm), indicating that the generated grasps were not only successful but also physically realistic.

![Generated Grasp Samples](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06589v1/x4.png)
*Figure 3: Samples of generated grasps demonstrating the framework's ability to handle diverse geometries and large scales.*

### Generalization and Adaptability
One of the strengths of this work is its ability to generalize. The data synthesis pipeline was successfully applied to different hand models, such as the Leap Hand, without structural changes. This suggests the principles of bimanual coordination and decoupled optimization are hand-agnostic.

### Real-World Deployment
The framework was deployed on two different physical robotic systems:
1.  A dual-arm setup with Piper arms and Brainco dexterous hands.
2.  A Unitree G1 humanoid robot with Inspire hands.

![Real World Experiments](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06589v1/x8.png)
*Figure 4: Real-world experimental setup using the Unitree G1 robot and dual Piper arms to grasp unseen objects.*

Despite being trained entirely in simulation, the model successfully generalized to real-world objects including a stool, a large plush watermelon, and a storage bin. Success rates in the real world ranged from $33.3\%$ to $100\%$, depending on the object complexity and the specific hardware used. The experiments confirmed that the scale-adaptive anchors and coordination modules are effective in bridging the gap between simulation and reality.

## Summary of Contributions

BiDexGrasp provides a comprehensive approach to bimanual dexterous grasping by addressing the two most significant bottlenecks in the field: the lack of diverse, large-scale data and the difficulty of coordinating high-dimensional action spaces across different scales.

By introducing a dataset of 9.7 million grasps and a framework that explicitly models bimanual coordination and scale adaptation, this work establishes a baseline for future research. The move away from absolute pose prediction toward relative, anchor-based regression, combined with decoupled optimization for data synthesis, offers a practical path toward robots that can reliably manipulate the large and complex objects found in human environments.

keyInsights: Decoupling force closure and employing region-constrained initialization are critical for efficient and high-quality bimanual grasp data synthesis.
Adopting scale-adaptive grasp anchors and geometry-adaptive features allows generative models to effectively generalize bimanual grasping across objects with diverse sizes and complex geometries.
A diffusion model framework, when augmented with specific modules for bimanual coordination and geometry-size adaptation, can generate robust and physically feasible bimanual grasps.
originalProblem: Lack of large-scale, high-quality bimanual dexterous grasp datasets covering diverse object geometries and sizes.
Current bimanual grasping methods perform inadequately on objects with varied scales and complex geometries due to difficulties in bimanual coordination and the expanded search space.
The high dimensionality of bimanual grasping makes efficient data synthesis and model learning challenging, leading to low success rates and slow speeds for existing pipelines.
results: The data synthesis pipeline improved grasp success rate by 2.8x and synthesis speed by 40x compared to existing bimanual grasp generation methods.
The BiDexGrasp generation framework achieved a 66.78% lift success rate in simulation, surpassing SceneDiffuser (15.17%) and DGTR (41.45%), with lower penetration depths.

Real-world deployment demonstrated the framework's capability to generate robust bimanual grasps for unseen objects, achieving success rates between 33.3% and 100% across various objects and robotic hands.
solution: Developed BiDexGrasp, a large-scale dataset of 9.7 million bimanual dexterous grasps for 6,351 objects of diverse sizes (30-80cm) and geometries, synthesized using a novel, efficient pipeline.
Designed a data-driven generative framework featuring a Bimanual Coordination Module to ensure collaborative hand movements and avoid collisions.
Incorporated a Geometry-Size-Adaptive strategy using Scale-Adaptive Grasp Anchors and Geometry-Adaptive Features to improve generalization across varied object characteristics.
summary.summary: Researchers developed BiDexGrasp, a large-scale dataset featuring 9.7 million bimanual dexterous grasps for 6,351 objects of varied sizes and geometries, alongside a generative framework for coordinated bimanual grasping. This framework achieves significantly higher grasp success rates (66.78%) and efficiency compared to previous methods, enabling robots to handle diverse objects in both simulation and real-world scenarios.

title: BiDexGrasp: Coordinated Bimanual Dexterous Grasps across Object Geometries and Sizes
